coredata.dc:title: Multi sensing functions integrated into one carbon-dot based platform via different types of mechanisms
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chun
Liu, Weijian
Sun, Xiaobo
Pan, Wei
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: Multi-mode sensing achieved by one sensor, which can be manufactured cost-effectively and facilely, is considerably attractive for analysts. In this work, triple sensing modes for Fe3+, Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid (AA) were integrated into one carbon-dot-based platform via different types of mechanisms. Firstly, the highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) which would act as the triple-mode platform was prepared by a simple hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid and acrylamide at 180°C for 10h, with a high quantum yield being 55.4%. In HAc-NaAc buffer solution, the CDs was selectively quenched by Fe3+ ascribed to specific binding affinity causing photo-induced electron transfer (PET). However, the interference of Fe3+ on the CDs was masked in PBS solution, and the fluorescence of the CDs was gradually turned off with the concentration of Cr(VI) increasing due to the inner filter effect (IFE). On the other hand, the fluorescence of the CDs/Cr(VI) system was increasingly recovered by AA owing to the elimination of IFE (EIFE) resulting from the reduction of Cr(VI). Under the optimized conditions, the linear ranges for the selective detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA were found to be 1–40, 2–180 and 8–160μM, with the limits of detections as low as 0.87, 2.10 and 0.91μM, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed methods were successfully applied to directly detect Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples (Fe3+ in tap water and lake water, Cr(VI) in lake water and industrial water and AA in Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine) with high accuracy. Interestingly, AA excreted in human urine after oral administration of AA tablets was fluorescently monitored in real-time by the CDs/Cr(VI) system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-mode sensing
Fe3+
Cr(VI)
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in aqueous solution
Detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
Triple sensing modes for Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA integrated into one CD-based sensor
Detection of Cr(VI) via IFE effect
Detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Triple sensing modes for Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA are achieved by one CD-based platform via different types of mechanisms including PET, IFE and EIFE.
(A) TEM image of the CDs. (B) Particle size distribution of the CDs.
(A) XRD pattern of the CDs. (B) FT-IR spectrum of the CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and photoluminescence spectra (excitation wavelengths as indicated with 20nm increments) of the as-prepared CDs. (B) Photostability test of the as-synthesized CDs in a fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 150W Xe lamp under 340nm excitation. (C) The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs emitted at 420nm (pH 5.0, the concentration of NaCl is 0, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400mM, respectively). (D) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at emission of 420nm.

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity at λex =340nm of the CDs in the presence of various metal ions in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 5.0) with all the concentrations of the metal ions being 100μM. (B) Fluorescence intensity reversibility of the CDs in HAc-NaAc buffer solution with addition of EDTA and the Fe3+. (C)Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 5.0) upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0–300μM, λex =340nm). (D) The relationship between the (F0-F)/F0 and Fe3+ concentrations (1–40μM) by fitting to the Stern–Volmer equation.

(A) UV−vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. (B) The effects of metal ions and oxidants (200μM) on the fluorescence of the CDs in PBS solution (pH 5.0, 0.1M). (C) FL intensity response of the CDs upon the addition of various concentrations of chromium ions (from top to bottom, 0–1.2mM) in PBS solution (pH 5.0, 0.1M). (D) The linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentration in the range of 2–180μM.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs, CDs/AA, CDs/Cr(VI) and CDs/Cr(VI)/AA in PBS solution (pH 5.0, 0.1M); The inset shows the corresponding photos of the CDs, CDs/Cr(VI) and CDs/Cr(VI)/AA in PBS solution illuminated by an UV beam of 365nm. (B) The selectivity of CDs/Cr(VI) system to AA in the presence of Gly, Glu, His, H2O2, urea, Glc, Fru, Lac, and EG, and all their concentrations are 200μM. (C)The fluorescence emission recovery of the CDs/Cr(VI) system upon the addition of AA with its concentration in the range of 0–600μM. (D) The linear relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and the concentration of AA.
Excretion profile (A) and photos (B) of AA excreted in human urine after oral administration of AA tablets.
Analytical results for the detection of Fe3+ in tap water and lake water samples.
Analytical results for the detection of Cr(VI) in lake water and industrial wastewater samples.
Analytical results for the detection of AA in actinidia chinensis planch and human urine.

head.ce:title: Multi sensing functions integrated into one carbon-dot based platform via different types of mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-mode sensing achieved by one sensor, which can be manufactured cost-effectively and facilely, is considerably attractive for analysts. In this work, triple sensing modes for Fe3+, Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid (AA) were integrated into one carbon-dot-based platform via different types of mechanisms. Firstly, the highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) which would act as the triple-mode platform was prepared by a simple hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid and acrylamide at 180°C for 10h, with a high quantum yield being 55.4%. In HAc-NaAc buffer solution, the CDs was selectively quenched by Fe3+ ascribed to specific binding affinity causing photo-induced electron transfer (PET). However, the interference of Fe3+ on the CDs was masked in PBS solution, and the fluorescence of the CDs was gradually turned off with the concentration of Cr(VI) increasing due to the inner filter effect (IFE). On the other hand, the fluorescence of the CDs/Cr(VI) system was increasingly recovered by AA owing to the elimination of IFE (EIFE) resulting from the reduction of Cr(VI). Under the optimized conditions, the linear ranges for the selective detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA were found to be 1–40, 2–180 and 8–160μM, with the limits of detections as low as 0.87, 2.10 and 0.91μM, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed methods were successfully applied to directly detect Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples (Fe3+ in tap water and lake water, Cr(VI) in lake water and industrial water and AA in Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine) with high accuracy. Interestingly, AA excreted in human urine after oral administration of AA tablets was fluorescently monitored in real-time by the CDs/Cr(VI) system.

ce:section.ce:para: Multi-mode sensing achieved by one chemosensor with high selectivity, sensitivity and accuracy, which might be manufactured cost-effectively and facilely with easily obtained source, is considerably attractive for analysts. Recently, a new emerging carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs), might fulfill such requirement, because they have high optical performance [1], and can be prepared by easily available and cheap precursors via facile synthesis approaches. Bright photoluminescence [2], as well as excellent photostability [3], favorable biocompatibility and low toxicity [4], are widely believed to be the high performance of CDs. To date, more and more materials which are easily available and cheap, such as citric acid [2], amino acid [5], wide natural sources including orange juices [6] and apple juices [7], grape peels [8], watermelon peels [9] and vegetables [10], had been reported as precursors to prepare CDs with high performance. Combined with facile preparation methods, commonly including electrochemical oxidation [11], combustion/thermal [12], microwave heating [13], chemical oxidation [14], hydrothermal carbonization [15], and pyrolysis [16], CDs can be obtained considerably cost-effectively. In addition, the response signals of CDs to analytes may originate from fluorescence quenching [17] and enhancement [18] via different mechanisms, as well as surface engineering will endow CDs more functions [19]. Multi sensing modes can be, therefore, integrated into one CD-based sensor manufactured cost-effectively and facilely.

Most of the CD-based sensors had been reported for single-mode sensing, such as Hg2+ [3,15], Cu2+ [10,14], Fe3+ [2], Ag+ [12,18], H2O2 [17], HOCl [20], Methylene blue [21], NO2 [22], O2 − [23] and tetracycline [13]. Dual-mode sensing by one CD-based sensor had been reported by a few studies. For example, two types of metal ions, Fe3+ and Ag+, were selectively detected by one chemosensor based on fluorescent carbon dots [24]. Fe3+ and Cd2+ were individually measured by a CD-based sensor synthesized by reflux of cysteine in concentrated H2SO4 [25]. Qu’s group [26] employed one CD-based sensor prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine to successfully detect Fe3+ and dopamine in aqueous solution. The CDs from pyrolysis of Assam tea was also reported as a dual-mode sensor to detect dopamine and ascorbic acid [27]. Triple-mode sensing for metal ions by one CD-based sensor had been reported by our group, in which the selectivity of the CDs capped with COO− can be switched by different buffer solutions, and the CD-based sensor was successfully applied to the individual detection of Fe3+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions in mineral water and tap water samples via PET mechanism [28]. A new type of dual-emission CDs prepared via one-step thermal decomposition of AA and alcohol had been performed as a highly efficient and sensitive triple-mode sensor for the selective detection of Fe3+, Bi3+ and Al3+ [29].

In this work, another new triple-mode sensing for Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in aqueous solution using one CD-based sensor via different mechanisms was described. Iron is the most essential trace metal element in all living organisms, which plays important roles in many chemical and biological processes in living organism, such as the transport of oxygen, storage metabolism, electron transfer in enzymatic reaction and in mitochondrial respiratory chain. Its deficiency or overloading in human body can disturb cellular homeostasis and metabolism, probably resulting in hepatitis, cancer, neurodegeneration, Alzheimer’s disease and Parkinson’s disease [30,31]. Cr(VI) is known to be seriously hazardous because it has acutely carcinogenic, mutagenic, teratogenic effects on human beings [32]. AA is an important vitamin (VitaminC) and a natural antioxidant in the human body. It plays crucial roles in biological activities, such as iron uptake in human intestinal cells, collagen synthesis, immune responses, and organism formation such as ligaments, blood vessels, cartilage, bones, and tendons, and treatments for scurvy and cancer [33,34].

It is of great interest to establish cheap and facile methods to detect Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA with high selectivity and sensitivity. Common approaches for the detection of Fe3+ and Cr(VI) are UV–vis spectroscopy, atomic absorption spectrometry(AAS), electrochemistry, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission(ICP-AES) [35–37]. And analytical methods for AA generally include capillary electrophoresis, electrochemistry, liquid chromatography, fluorescence, spectrophotometry and chemiluminescence (CL) [38–40]. To integrate different approaches that might need expensive devices and complicated processes for the detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA into one CD-based sensor is considerably significant.

In this work, highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) with a high quantum yield of 55.4% was facilely prepared by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and acrylamide as precursors. Notably, the fluorescence of the CDs can be selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions in HAc-NaAc solution at pH=5.0, attributed to photo-induced electron transfer (PET) caused by the specific interactions between Fe3+ and functional groups on the surface of the CDs. And its excitation peak at 340 and emission peak centered at 420nm can be overlapped by the absorption bands centered at 350 and 440nm of Cr(VI), resulting in the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence of the CDs in a highly efficient way. However, Cr(VI) can be reduced by AA, and the IFE of Cr(VI) on the CDs will be eliminated upon the addition of AA, resulting in an increase in fluorescence of the CDs/Cr(VI) system. Accordingly, it inspired us to develop a method to detect Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA using one CD-based platform via different types of mechanisms including PET, IFE and elimination of IFE (EIFE), as shown in Scheme 1 . The proposed method was successfully applied to directly detect Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples (Fe3+ in tap water and lake water, Cr(VI) in lake water and industrial water and AA in Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine). Although the components of the real samples are very complicated, the spiked Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA can be recovered from these samples with high accuracy. It suggests that the proposed method is highly selective. Interestingly, AA excreted in human urine after oral administration of AA tablets was fluorescently monitored in real-time by the CDs/Cr(VI) system.

AgNO3, CaCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, Fe(NO3)3, Hg(Ac)2, MgCl2, MnSO4, Pb(NO3)2, AlCl3, BaCl2, CrCl3, NaCl, LiNO3, KCl and Zn(NO3)2 were purchased from Damao Chemical Corp (Tianjin, China). Citric acid, Acetic Acid, Sodium acetate trihydrate, Glycine (Gly), Glutamic acid (Glu), Histidine (His), H2O2, Urea, Glucose (Glc), Fructose (Fru), Lactose (Lac), Ethylene glycol (EG), NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4 and Acrylamide were obtained from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3MΩcm). Tap water, lake water, urine and Actinidia chinensis Planch were used as the real samples. Generally, tap water contains a lot of cations and anions [41]. Besides cations and anions, there are many organic matters present in lake water [42]. The components of urine include nitrogen-containing products of protein decay, and also inorganic salts [43]. Actinidia chinensis Planch has plenty of AA, and its components are much more complex.

The morphology and size of CDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi HT-7700, operated at 80kV). Fluorescent spectra were performed by a fluorescence spectrometer F-4600 (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the carbon dots was collected using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu-Kα radiation (40kV, 40mA, λ=1.5418Å) at a scanning rate of 1° min−1 in the range from 10° to 80°. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the CDs was recorded using a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo, America) using KBr pellets technique, over the ranging from 400 to 4000cm−1. The quantum yields (QYs) of the prepared carbon dots were determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature QY: 54.6%) as the standard sample by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 360nm) and absorbance values at 360nm of the carbon dots aqueous solutions with those of quinine sulfat [15,44].

The CDs was synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. Briefly, 200mg citric acid and 400mg acrylamide were dissolved with 25mL ultrapure water in a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and then heated at 180°C for 10h. The resultant solution was purified by gel column (G-25) with ultrapure water as the eluent, and the wavelength of 365nm was used to monitor the fraction eluted from the column. The solid powder of the CDs was collected from the fractions by lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Fe3+ and Cr(VI), Fe3+ was added into HAc-NaAc buffer solution, and Cr(VI) into PBS solution of the CDs (0.03mgmL−1, pH 5.0) at various concentrations, then their fluorescence spectra were recorded after reaction for 20min. For the detection of AA, CDs/Cr(VI) system was established by mixing CDs (0.03mgmL−1) and Cr(VI) (10μM) in PBS solution, thereafter, AA was added into CDs/Cr(VI) system at different concentrations, and then the recovered fluorescence spectra were measured under excitation at 340nm. The fluorescence intensity at 420nm was used to fabricate the linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the CDs based sensor for the detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples, quantitative analysis of Fe3+ in the tap water and lake water samples, Cr(VI) in lake water and industrial waste water samples, and AA in Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine, were performed using the present CD-based sensor. The lake water samples were obtained from the Hongzi Lake in the campus of Qingdao Agricultural University and tap water samples from our lab. The industrial waste water samples were obtained from Yimen Copper Co., Ltd. Actinidia chinensis Planch were purchased from a local supermarket, and human urine samples were obtained from healthy male volunteers in our laboratory.

The tap water samples without any pretreatment were spiked with Fe3+. And the lake water and industrial waste water samples were pretreated by qualitative filter paper and 0.22μm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities. The resultant lake water samples were spiked with the different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cr(VI), respectively. 6.6g fruit of Actinidia chinensis Planch was mashed and filtered by etamine. The filtrate of Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine were both treated by 0.22μm filter membrane. After original AA in the filtrates of Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine was measured, exogenetic AA at different concentrations was added into the samples and detected using the proposed methods.

For real-time monitoring AA in human urine, the healthy male volunteers were administrated five tablets of AA (500mg) in the morning with empty stomach. Urine samples were collected in dark glass bottles after 0–6, 8, 10, 12, 14, and 24h of oral administration of AA tablets. Then AA in human urine was detected by the proposed method in real time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The highly luminescent CDs was facilely prepared by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and acrylamide as precursors. The CDs prepared under the optimum condition (ncitric acid: nacrylamide =1:2 at 180°C for 10h) presented a maximum quantum yield of 55.4% using quinine sulfate (quantum yield=54.6%) as a standard at an excitation wavelength of 340nm.
The morphology of the CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), demonstrating the uniform and monodisperse nature of the CDs with an average diameter of 8.6nm (see Fig. 1 ).
The XRD pattern depicted in Fig. 2 A shows a broad diffraction peak (2θ) at 21.29° (d002 =0.42nm), attributing to graphite crystalline of the CDs [45].

Generally, FTIR spectrum is used to identify the surface groups of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the broad bands at 3200–3550cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The peaks centered at 1687 and 1573cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO and the bending vibrations of the NH, indicating the presence of amide groups. The absorption at 1403cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of CN [6].

The optical properties of the CDs in the dilute aqueous solution were investigated by UV and fluorescence spectra as shown in Fig. 3 A. There is a distinct absorption peak centered at 248nm attributed to the π→π* transition [2]. The weak absorption band at 300–400nm may be contributed by the n→π* transition [3]. A series of emission spectra of the CDs were recorded at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400nm (Fig. 3A). The emission of the as-prepared CDs exhibited independence on the excitation wavelength, which might result from its unique surface states and the size distribution.

The photostability of the CDs was investigated by continuous irradiation with a beam of 340nm UV light in aqueous solution (0.03 mg·mL−1). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs was found to change negligibly after 60min irradiation, suggesting good photostability of the CDs (see Fig. 3B). In order to evaluate the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence of the CDs, its intensity of the CDs in NaCl solutions at different concentrations was recorded as shown in Fig. 3C, which did not change as the concentration of NaCl increased from 0 to 400mM, indicating that the detection will not suffer from the variation of ionic strength in solution. The influence of pH on the emission of the CDs was also tested. As demonstrated in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged in the pH range of 3.0–7.0, which is the suitable range for the detections. Based on our previous work [28], HAc-NaAc solution can mask some metal ions resulting in selective detection of Fe3+. And Fe3+ can be masked in PBS solution, which is profitable for the selective detection of Cr(VI) in the subsequent measurements. HAc-NaAc and PBS have ability to buffer the solutions in the pH range of 3.0–7.0. And the buffer effective range of the HAc-NaAc solution is 3.74–5.74 (pKa±1), the optimum pH condition for the redox reaction between AA and Cr(VI) is around 5 [46]. Therefore, pH 5.0 was selected as the optimum pH condition for the subsequent detections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triple sensing modes for Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA integrated into one CD-based sensor

ce:section.ce:para: From the evaluation of the effects of metal ions, including Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Li+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, K+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Na+ and Ag+, on the fluorescence of the CDs in HAc-NaAc buffer at pH 5.0 under excitation at 340nm, it can be seen that only Fe3+ can significantly quench the fluorescence of the CDs while other metal ions nearly exerted no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs (Fig. 4 A). The CDs, therefore, have a potential to be performed as a sensor for selective detection of Fe3+ in HAc-NaAc buffer solution.

The mechanism that Fe3+ ions caused fluorescence quenching of the CDs in HAc-NaAc solution at pH=5.0 can be attributed to the specific interactions between Fe3+ and functional groups (–OH and amide groups identified by the FTIR spectrum) on the surface of the CDs (forming non-fluorescent complexes). It was confirmed by the fact that the fluorescence intensity could be recovered when EDTA, a competitive chelator, was added to the CDs solutions quenched by Fe3+ (Fig. 4B). Due to this coordination interaction, electrons in the excited state of the CDs would transfer to the unfilled orbitals of the metal ions and lead to photo-induced electron transfer (PET), which resulted in fluorescence quenching.

The sensitivity of the CDs to Fe3+ was investigated by recording the emission spectra at different concentrations in pH=5.0 HAc-NaAc buffer solution under excitation at 340nm (Fig. 4C). Upon the addition of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 300μM, the fluorescence intensity emitted at 420nm gradually decreased. There was an excellent linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (F0–F)/F0 and the concentration of Fe3+ in the range from 1.0 to 40.0μM (Fig. 4D) with calibration equation as follows: (F0–F)/F0 =0.0094c+0.0357 (R2 =0.9986), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 420nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, and c (molL−1) represents the concentration of Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ ions was evaluated to be 0.87μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD=3Sd/s). In summary, the CDs has a potential to be as a sensor for the detection of Fe3+ via PET mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) via IFE effect
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 A, there are three absorption bands centered at 260, 350 and 440nm in the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) solution. On the other hand, two bands at 250 and 340nm are presented in the excitation spectrum, and a peak centered at 420nm in the emission spectrum of the CDs under the excitation at 340nm. As a result, Cr(VI) can not only shield the excitation light on the CDs but also absorb the emission light of the CDs, which ensures that the IFE of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence of the CDs occurs in a highly efficient way.

More interestingly, in the HAc-NaAc buffer solution the fluorescence of the CDs was selectively quenched by Fe3+, however, in the PBS solution Fe3+ did not exert significant effect on the CDs, which might result from the masking effect that Fe3+ formed more stable complex with the main components of the PBS solution. Besides Fe3+ ion, all the tested metal ions, including Fe2+, Co2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Li+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, K+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Na+ and Ag+, showed no obvious interferences on the fluorescence of the CDs. Only Cr(VI) can turn off the fluorescence of the CDs via IFE with high selectivity.

In order to identify whether the fluorescence quenching of the CDs originated from the surface oxidization of the CDs by Cr(VI), more oxidants, such as KMnO4, H2O2, KClO3 and KClO4, were selected for further investigation. As shown in Fig. 5B, KMnO4, H2O2, KClO3 and KClO4 at the concentration of 100μM nearly did not cause any decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in PBS solution (0.2M, pH=5.0). It is confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of the CDs resulted from IFE, other than oxidation.

The sensitivity of the CDs to the concentration of Cr(VI) via IFE was studied, and the results were shown in Fig. 5C. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased as the concentration of Cr(VI) increased from 0.01μM to 1.2mM. A good linear relationship was exhibited between (F0–F)/F0 and the concentration of Cr(VI) in the range from 2 to 180μM in PBS solution (pH=5), as presented in Fig. 5D. The linear equation was regressed as: (F0–F)/F0 =0.0029c+0.0747 (R2 =0.9957), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively, c represents the concentration of Cr(VI). The LOD of the synthesized CDs was estimated to be 2.1μM. Therefore, the CDs can be developed as a sensor for the selective detection of Cr(VI) via IFE.

Since Cr(VI) can be reduced by AA following the reaction: 3AA+Cr2O7 2− + 8H+ =3DHA+2Cr3+ +7H2O, the IFE of Cr(VI) on the CDs will be eliminated upon the addition of AA, resulting in an increase in fluorescence of the CDs/Cr(VI) system. As a result, the CDs/Cr(VI) system is then proposed to be designed as a potential sensing platform for AA via EIFE.

As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence of the CDs/Cr(VI) system was recovered by AA, while AA itself exerted negligible effect on the emission of the CDs. The effects of Gly, Glu, His, H2O2, Urea, Glc, Fru, Lac and EG on the fluorescence recovery were studied so as to evaluate the selectivity of the CDs/Cr(VI) system to AA. As depicted in Fig. 6B, the CDs/Cr(VI) system can selectively respond to AA even in the presence of the above molecules.

The sensitivity of the CDs/Cr(VI) system to AA was also investigated by recording the fluorescence spectra with the concentration of AA increasing. As shown in Fig. 6C, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/Cr(VI) system gradually increased upon the addition of AA. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F−F0)/F0 showed an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of AA in the range of 8–160μM. The linear equation could be fitted as follows: (F−F0)/F0 =0.0085c+0.3538 (R2 =0.9964), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs/Cr(VI) system in the absence and presence of AA, respectively. c represents the concentration of AA. The LOD for sensing AA was calculated to be as low as 0.91μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high sensitivity and selectivity, the proposed methods based on the CDs can be directly applied for detecting Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples. To evaluate the feasibility of the CDs based sensor for the detection of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples, quantitative analysis of Fe3+ in the tap water and lake water samples, Cr(VI) in lake water and industrial waste water samples, and AA in Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine, were performed using the present CD-based sensor. The tap water samples without any pretreatment were spiked with Fe3+. And the lake water and industrial waste water samples were pretreated by qualitative filter paper and 0.22μm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities. The resultant lake water samples were spiked with the different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cr(VI), respectively. The treated industrial waste water samples were spiked with the different concentrations of Cr(VI). The lake water samples were obtained from the Hongzi Lake in the campus of Qingdao Agricultural University and tap water samples from our lab. Actinidia chinensis Planch were purchased from a local supermarket, and human urine samples were obtained from healthy male volunteers in our laboratory. Fresh fruit of Actinidia chinensis Planch was mashed and filtered by etamine, then the filtrate of Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine were treated by 0.22μm filter membrane. After original AA in the filtrates of Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine was measured, additional AA with different concentrations was added into the samples and detected using the proposed methods.

As presented in Tables 1–3
, the recoveries for the selective detection of Fe3+ in the tap water and lake water samples were in the range 97.00% to 101.91% and 98.37% to 102.53%, and Cr(VI) ions in the lake water and industrial waste water samples in the range of 97.60% to 104.95%, and 97.85% to 103.28%, and AA in Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine samples in the range of 96.29% to 102.01% and 101.57% to 108.20%.

Tap water contains a lot of cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe3+ and Al3+) and anions (SO4 2−, HCO3 − NO3 − and PO4 3−) [40]. Besides the cations and anions as in tap water, there are many organic matters including humic substances (fulvic and humic acids), carbohydrates, proteins, amino acids, lipids, phenols, alcohols, organic acids and sterols presented in the lake water [41]. The components of urine include nitrogen-containing products of protein decay (urea, uric and hippuric acids, and creatinine), amino acids, glucose, protein, etc., and also inorganic salts (mainly chlorides, sulphates, and phosphates of potassium, sodium, ammonium, calcium, and magnesium) [42]. Actinidia chinensis Planch has plenty of AA, and its components are much more complex. Although the components of the real samples are very complicated and they may interfere the measurement, the spiked Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA can be recovered from these samples with accuracy, which indicates that the proposed methods are of high selectivity.

The as-proposed method was further applied to fluorescently monitor AA excretion in human urine in real time after oral administration of AA tablets. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the excretion of AA in urine gradually increased after oral administration of AA tablets, and reached the maximum in 4h, and then decreased rapidly in another 10h. The excretion of AA within 24h was 19.09% of the total amount of oral administration. Interestingly, it allows us to observe the process of gradual emission recovery with AA excretion in human urine with naked eyes (Fig. 7B).

In this work, triple sensing modes for Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA can be achieved by one CD-based platform via different types of mechanisms including PET, IFE and EIFE. And the carbon-dot-based platform was manufactured cost-effectively and facilely with easily obtained source, owning a high quantum yield of 55.4%. The as-fabricated carbon-dot-based platform were successfully applied to directly detect Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in real samples (Fe3+ in tap water and lake water, Cr(VI) in lake water and industrial water and AA in Actinidia chinensis Planch and human urine). Furthermore, it is of great interest that AA excretion in human urine after oral administration of AA tablets was monitored in real-time by means of the CDs/Cr(VI) system. In conclusion, multi sensing functions, which might need different devices and complicated processes, can be integrated into one carbon-dot based platform via different types of mechanism.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181), Project for Science and Technology Development of Shandong Province (No. 2014GSF121034, 2016GSF116007), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2014CM012), Key Research and Development Project of Shandong Province (No. 2016GSF116007).

Graphitic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of Fe3+ ions
Sensitive and selective detection of silver(I) ion in aqueous solution using carbon nanoparticles as a cheap, effective fluorescent sensing platform
Carbon dots derived from rose flowers for tetracycline sensing

Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions

Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dots and thin graphene sheets for non-enzymatic sensing of hydrogen peroxide

Visualizing gaseous nitrogen dioxide by ratiometric fluorescence of carbon nanodots-quantum dots hybrid

Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-Free detection of Iron(III) ions and dopamine, chem
Carbon dot based sensing of dopamine and ascorbic acid
The selectivity of the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots switched by buffer solutions for the detection of multi-metal ions

Malabsorption and defective utilization of iron in three siblings

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: K.M.G. Mostofa, C. Liu, M.A. Mottaleb, G. Wan, H. Ogawa, D. Vione, et al. Dissolved Organic Matter in Natural Waters, 2013, 1–137.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cloud point extraction utilizable for separation and preconcentration of (ultra)trace elements in biological fluids before their determination by spectrometric methods: a brief review
Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield of quantum dots: suitable procedures and achievable uncertainties
Green synthesized carbon nanodots as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of iron(III) ions
A review of chemical, electrochemical and biological methods for aqueous Cr(VI) reduction

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chun Li was born in Zhaodong City, Heilongjiang Province, China. He received his BSc from Kunming University of Science and Technology in 2014. He joined Prof. Jinping Wang’s group at Qingdao Agricultural University as a graduate student in 2015. His current research focuses on synthesis of carbon dots and their applications in biosensors and bioimaging.

Weijian Liu received her BSc in Material Chemistry from Qingdao Agricultural University in 2015. She is currently a graduate student at Qingdao Agricultural University, majoring in applied chemistry. Her research interests are primarily in preparation of fluorescent probes, and their applications in analytical chemistry.

Xiaobo Sun received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2005. Now she is an Associate Professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University, China. Her research interests are primarily in analytical chemistry and separation science.

Wei Pan was born in Shandong, China, in 1979. He received his MSc from Sichuan University in 2006. Then he joined Qingdao Agricultural University in Sep. 2006 as an experimentalist. His research interests focus on functional nanomaterials and devices.

Jinping Wang received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2004. He received postdoctoral training at Waseda University in 2007–2009, and worked as a visiting scholar at Clemson University in 2012–2013. Now he is a Full Professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University, China. His research interests are primarily in preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials, and their bioimaging and sensing applications.
